paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: This podcast is about highly practical philosophy. If you live long enough, you become a philosopher because you start trying to solve the big problems: How do I stay happy? How do I become healthy? How do I become wealthy? This is practical philosophy that we've confirmed for ourselves. What one monkey can do, another can. If this person learned something that made them happier, healthier, or wealthier, then I can do it too. We focus on the practical philosophy of health, wealth, and happiness. If you were going to sum it up in one word, it would just be health. Financial health, mental health, physical health. Health is just the state of being well.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Financial wealth can give us freedom. It can give us time. It can give us peace. You're not going to buy your way to happiness, but you will buy your way out of common causes of unhappiness. In olden times, one route to finding peace was becoming a monk. You would renounce sex, money, shelter, attachments, and go off in the woods. After 30 years you might find some peace. But the truth is most of them probably never got over it. Lots of monks out there, not a lot of enlightened people. Whatever it is that you deny yourself will become your new prison. If you have a desire for material comfort, it's actually going to be easier and faster to fulfill it in modern society than it will be for you to renounce it.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Physical health is the foundation of everything. Without your physical health, you have nothing. A sick man only wants one thing. A healthy man wants 10,000 things. I speak the least on that topic because I have the least specific and unique knowledge. I'm in okay health, okay shape, and I have an okay diet. I would feel fraudulent giving you that theory unless I were a paragon of excellence in that domain. Whereas in wealth, I'm much more self-actualized. On the peace side, I've gone from being a mostly unhappy person to being a very happy person. That was deliberate. It was practiced. It was effort-based. It was through realizations, and I made progress, so I have a chance to tell you what worked for me.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Happiness is an extremely loaded term. It means completely different things to different people. I'm going to conflate happiness and pleasure and peace and joy and bliss. This is not math. We cannot clearly bound these words. They mean different things in different contexts to different people. Let's just try and get into the spirit of what I'm saying rather than get hung up on specific words and details. When some people talk about happiness, they're really talking about pleasure thrills. Other people are talking about a general state of contentment and well-being. Other people are referring to enlightenment like a Buddha would have reached. Don't fixate on the words. Don't even fixate on the sentences. Ponder the overall thought process and message.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: The first step towards increasing the level of happiness in your life is realizing that you can. For the vast majority of people, some amount of their happiness, in fact probably a lot more than they think, is in their control. The first step is not easy. Many people deep down believe that happiness cannot be developed or changed, and so they devalue it. Genetics may account for half or more than half of things like strength, athletic performance, and intelligence, but your genetic set point is only about half of it, and happiness or general contentment is much more malleable than the other things. In terms of my mood and temperament and my outlook on life, am I a peaceful person or not? How angry do I get? These things are much more malleable. Happiness is a skill like nutrition and fitness. It is a skill that you identify and develop until you get better and better at it.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Desire is a contract you make with yourself to be unhappy until you get what you want. You start becoming disturbed because you want something, and then you work really hard to get that thing, and you're miserable in the meantime, and then when you get that thing, you actually revert to the state you were in before you had that thing. There's a delusion that there's something out there that will make me happy and fulfilled forever. That is a complete delusion. No one thing seems to do that.
